text: Welcome everyone to today's lecture on Introduction to Machine Learning.
Machine Learning is a subset of Artificial Intelligence where systems learn from data without being explicitly programmed.
There are three main types: supervised learning, unsupervised learning, and reinforcement learning.
In supervised learning, we train models on labeled data — meaning each example has an input and a known correct output.
Common supervised learning algorithms include Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, and Support Vector Machines.
Unsupervised learning deals with unlabeled data. The model tries to find hidden patterns or groupings by itself.
K-Means Clustering and Principal Component Analysis, or PCA, are popular unsupervised learning techniques.
Reinforcement learning is inspired by behavioural psychology. An agent learns by interacting with an environment and receiving rewards or penalties.
Now let us talk about the concept of a model. A model is a mathematical function that takes inputs and produces predictions.
The process of adjusting model parameters to minimise error is called training. We use a loss function to measure how wrong our predictions are.
Mean Squared Error, or MSE, is the most common loss function for regression problems.
For classification problems, we typically use Cross-Entropy Loss, also known as Log Loss.
Gradient Descent is the optimisation algorithm used to minimise the loss function. It iteratively updates weights in the direction of steepest descent.
The learning rate is a hyperparameter that controls how large each update step is. Too high and the model diverges; too low and training is very slow.
Overfitting occurs when a model performs well on training data but poorly on new, unseen data. It memorises instead of generalising.
Techniques to prevent overfitting include Regularisation — L1 and L2 — Dropout for neural networks, and cross-validation.
The bias-variance tradeoff is a fundamental concept. High bias leads to underfitting; high variance leads to overfitting.
A validation set is used to tune hyperparameters, while the test set gives the final unbiased evaluation of model performance.
Feature engineering is the process of using domain knowledge to select and transform raw data into features that improve model performance.
To summarise: Machine Learning enables computers to learn from data. Choosing the right algorithm, loss function, and regularisation strategy is key to building good models.
